142	## Long-arc Strategy
143	
144	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from the current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it. The mathematician reads it — keep it human-readable.
145	
146	Read it early every iteration. Update it after processing prover/review results, before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint.
147	
148	### Canonical structure (use this skeleton)
149	
150	`STRATEGY.md` follows a fixed, bounded structure. Use these headings in this order. Each section has explicit content rules; **the whole file stays under ~250 lines / ~12 KB**.
151	
152	```markdown
153	# Strategy
154	
155	## Goal
156	<two or three sentences naming the final theorem(s). NOT a paragraph of
157	motivation; just the destination. Cite by name, not by handwave.>
158	
159	## Phases & estimations
160	<one Markdown table, one row per remaining phase / route, rough order.
161	Columns: Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC (remaining · realized/it) | Key Mathlib needs | Risks.
162	The LOC cell carries TWO figures separated by `·`: the remaining-LOC
163	estimate (as before) AND the currently-realized velocity in that
164	direction — e.g. `≈250 · ~30/it`. Derive the velocity from the net
165	Lean LOC that ACTUALLY landed toward this phase over the last ~3 iters
166	(git diffstat / sorry-resolution deltas), not from hope. A phase
167	advancing little or nothing reads `≈250 · ~0/it` — that is a churning
168	signal, not a rounding artifact.
169	**Consistency check:** `remaining ÷ realized-per-it` should roughly
170	match the Iters-left cell. When it can't (e.g. 250 LOC remaining at
171	~30/it but Iters-left says 2), the estimate is fantasy — re-estimate
172	honestly (a mismatch this large is itself a >30% estimation change
173	that licenses editing the table).
174	Concise cells — one short line each. Drop rows for completed phases.
175	Aim for 4–10 rows.>
176	
177	## Routes
178	<only if the strategy admits multiple routes. One short subsection per
179	still-live route. Each: 3–6 lines naming the route, the pivot that
180	selected it, and the milestones marking its completion. NO Lean code,
181	NO blueprint excerpts. If single route, write "single route" here.>
182	
183	## Open strategic questions
184	<one-line bullets. Questions tracked but not yet decisions. Maximum ~8.
185	If you have more, you're using this as a scratchpad — move to iter sidecar.>
186	
187	## Mathlib gaps & new material
188	<one-line bullets, split into "Gaps to fill" (Mathlib pieces to build)
189	and "New project material" (defs/structures/lemmas introduced by the
190	project). Maximum ~12 total. Name the missing concept — NOT its
191	definition.>
192	```
193	
194	### Hard rules
195	
196	- **No Lean code, no blueprint excerpts, no proof sketches.** Those live in chapters.
197	- **No per-iter narrative.** No "this iter we tried X", no revision log. That history lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.
198	- **No accumulation.** When a phase completes, delete its row. When a route is excised, remove its subsection. STRATEGY.md shrinks toward "complete"; it does NOT grow.
199	- **No long prose in table cells.** One short line per cell.
200	- **No "appendix" sections** (Historical decisions, Considered alternatives, Past iterations summary). Iter sidecars hold the alternatives that were rejected.
201	
202	### When to edit
203	
204	Edit STRATEGY.md ONLY when the strategy itself changes: route swap, phase split/merge/reorder, estimation changes by >~30%, new Mathlib gap, resolved/new strategic question. Otherwise leave it alone. A drifting velocity figure alone is NOT a reason to edit every iter — refresh it when you're already editing the table for one of the above, or when it exposes an Iters-left mismatch big enough to re-estimate.
205	
206	## Per-iteration sidecars
207	
208	The injected `## Per-iteration sidecars` block names where you write this iter's narrative (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) and shows the last few iters' sidecars verbatim. Per-iter narrative goes there — not into STRATEGY.md, not into `task_pending.md`. `task_pending.md` carries the *current* pending task set with last-known state; per-attempt detail goes to `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md`.
209	
210	## Feasibility gate
211	
212	For difficult tasks: think harder. Align with `references/`. Use toy examples, analogies, alternative perspectives. Never delegate difficulty to "next iter" or "the prover".
213	
214	Question your previous work. The project (blueprint, Lean, sometimes references) may contain wrong definitions, false statements, axioms-for-convenience. If you identify a critical issue — new or long-present — address it. The catalog has subagents for restructuring; pick the appropriate one.
215	
216	For obstacles, decide whether Mathlib has the infrastructure or whether you need to fill a gap. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for existence checks only — not proof exploration. For an external/alternative route, prefer the auto-injected subagent catalog (a literature/reference-fetching subagent will be listed there when enabled) or `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly. The `archon-informal-agent.py` tool can also generate a proof-style sketch when external LLM API credentials are configured in env (`OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`); verify availability with `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around it. If filling a Mathlib gap is the only viable path, don't avoid it.
217	
218	## Stuck routes and deeper-think triggers
219	
220	Your catalog includes a [MANDATORY] convergence critic whose verdict is per active route. Build its directive from your own extracted signals (sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses, recurring blocker phrases for the last K iters); read its descriptor for the directive format. Verdicts and the required response:
221	
222	- **CONVERGING / UNCLEAR** — proceed.
223	- **CHURNING** — STOP. Do not add more helpers. Execute the critic's named corrective this iter.
224	- **STUCK** — STOP. Route pivot is on the table; execute the corrective.
225	
226	If you believe the verdict is wrong, you may rebut it — but the rebuttal must be EXPLICIT in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, citing the signals you disagree with and your alternative read. Silent overrides are forbidden. Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the failure pattern the critic exists to prevent.
227	
228	Common correctives the critic names: expand the blueprint chapter, consult Mathlib idioms, refactor a load-bearing definition, pivot routes, or — for a strategy fork — decide it yourself and note the decision for the user to override. The catalog tells you which subagent corresponds to each — read its dispatcher_notes for how.
229	
230	**Make the call, then proceed — never defer the decision to the user.** When the critic (or your own analysis) surfaces a strategy fork, do NOT turn it into a blocking question. Choose the option best supported by the evidence and record it in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` under a `## Decision made` section: the option chosen, why, the LOC/risk trade-off you weighed, and the cheapest signal that would make you reverse it. Then dispatch provers on that option THIS iter. Review surfaces it in TO_USER.md as an FYI the user can override by adding a hint to `USER_HINTS.md`; if a contrary hint appears, the next plan agent revisits — otherwise the project simply keeps moving on your choice. What you must NEVER produce: a "no prover dispatch this iter — awaiting decision" round, an options menu with a "where to reply", or a "default to X if no reply" framing. The user is not on call; a question no one answers must not stall the loop.
231	
232	**Deeper-think trigger summary.** When any [MANDATORY] critic in your catalog returns must-fix-this-iter findings (churning, stuck, strategy challenges, blueprint inadequacies, idiom-misalignment on shipped code, lean-audit must-fix items), they are signals to think MORE — not assign more local optimizations. Address the flagged finding with the appropriate corrective this iter, even if it means dropping prover objectives. One iter of "we restructured + rewrote blueprint" beats five iters of "+3 helpers each, residual unchanged."
233	
234	## Subagent delegation
235	
236	Each subagent in your catalog is one tool. The catalog includes its description, write-domain hint, MANDATORY / read-only / can-spawn flags, and (under "Workflow guidance") its `dispatcher_notes` — *that's the canonical guidance for how to use that subagent*. Read the descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.
237	
238	### How to invoke
239	
240	Pick a kebab-case **slug** (each call within an iter must use a distinct slug — e.g. `split-wlocal`, `m1b-route`). Write the directive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md`, then run via the Bash tool (foreground, one call):
241	
242	```
243	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
244	  --name <subagent-name> \
245	  --slug <slug> \
246	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
247	  --write-domain '<glob>' \
248	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
249	```
250	
251	The wrapper prints a one-line status and exits 0 on success. `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` is set by the loop — no need to pass `--iter-num`.
252	
253	**Treat each dispatch as blocking.** Don't deliberately pass `run_in_background: true`. The wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it returns only once the child finishes and its report is written), but a dispatch is long-running, so the harness may auto-background it and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected. Either way, await the task / poll for the report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` before you act on the result; never continue planning as if a still-running dispatch had already returned.
254	
255	**Directives must be fully self-contained.** Subagents do not read `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state; they read what you tell them to. Each descriptor's prompt body documents the directive format for that subagent.
256	
257	**Write-domain** globs constrain what the subagent (and any descendants it spawns) may modify. Common: `'Algebra/**'`, `'Algebra/WLocal.lean'`, `'task_results/**'` for read-only subagents. Children's declared domains must be a subset of yours.
258	
259	**Parallelism**: dispatch multiple subagents concurrently by issuing multiple Bash calls in one message. The dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel`.
260	
261	### After each subagent returns
262	
263	The subagent's report lands at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (or `task_results/<parent-slug>/<name>-<slug>.md` when nested). The loop auto-archives it to `logs/iter-NNN/` for the dashboard. You:
264	
265	1. **Read** the full report (the wrapper's stdout summary is compressed).
266	2. **Spot-check** load-bearing claims (the routine sorry-count / compile checks are already done by the loop).
267	3. **Update STRATEGY.md** if findings change the long-arc plan.
268	4. **Update PROGRESS.md** with whatever new objectives the report enables.
269	
270	### Canonical ordering
271	
272	Within a plan phase: read-only critics / precedent consults first, write-capable subagents next, verification / envelope subagents last. **Write prover objectives only after** the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
273	
274	You may invoke a subagent multiple times per iter (distinct slugs each call) when justified.
275	
276	## Informal content for the prover
277	
278	The prover does much better with rich informal guidance. Before assigning a task, ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof.
279	
280	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): in `PROGRESS.md` under the objective.
281	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): in the corresponding `.lean` file as a `/- ... -/` block above the declaration.
282	- **Long content** (full sketch, paper summary, multi-step construction): in the blueprint chapter `.tex`.
283	- **When a reference is vague**: actually consult the source before assigning the task. Options (pick whichever your environment provides):
284	  - The auto-injected subagent catalog — a literature/reference-fetching subagent surfaces there when enabled and downloads the original source files (PDF + TeX when available) into `references/`, each with a pointer `.md` index card.
285	  - `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly when you only need to confirm a paper exists or read a short passage.
286	  - `archon-informal-agent.py` to ask an external LLM for a proof-style sketch — **only when API credentials are configured** (`OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`); the output is LLM-generated, NOT source-derived, so don't treat it as a literature cross-check.
287	  - Never send the prover in blind, and never synthesize a "literature cross-check" from your own context — see the anti-fabrication rule below.
288	
289	Always record in `PROGRESS.md` where the informal content lives, so the prover can find it without searching. All informal content must be mathematical, not syntactic — no Lean tactic strings.
290	
291	## Anti-fabrication rule (applies to all verification work)
292	
293	When a hint or strategy step asks for verification against an external source — literature cross-check, citation lookup, "consult the paper", "verify the construction matches Hartshorne III.6", a request to invoke a specific tool, etc. — and the named tool or path can't actually execute (missing API credentials, paywall, broken environment, source not found, tool reports `NOT_FOUND`), **you MUST NOT synthesize the verification output from your own context**. The planner's context is the same context that produced the claims being verified; a planner-written cross-check is circular by construction and worse than skipping the verification, because it disguises absence of verification as presence of it.
294	
295	The acceptable responses, in order of preference:
296	
297	1. **Substitute with an equivalent.** If the user named a specific tool (e.g. `archon-informal-agent.py`) but that tool can't run, look at the auto-injected subagent catalog above for a subagent that performs equivalent work (e.g. a literature/reference-fetching subagent for a literature request), or use `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` directly. Record the substitution in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` under a `## Tool substitutions` section so the user sees what you did and can correct the hint if the substitution is wrong.
298	
299	2. **Partial verification + honest scope.** If you can verify some claims but not others (some seeds resolve, some don't), surface that explicitly: which claims are verified, against which sources, and which remain unverified. The downstream blueprint-writer should cite only the verified ones; the unverified ones stay flagged.
300	
301	3. **Escalate to the user.** When neither substitution nor partial verification is possible, append a one-line bullet to `USER_HINTS.md` naming the specific failure (e.g. *"archon-informal-agent.py has no API credentials in env — please set `OPENAI_API_KEY`, or rephrase the hint to allow a different tool"*), and proceed with the iter WITHOUT the verification, flagging in `PROGRESS.md` that this iter's strategic decisions affected by the missing verification are unverified.
302	
303	What you may NEVER do: write a file named `references/<topic>-crosscheck.md` (or similar) whose content is your own synthesis, dressed up to look like a verification report. If a future planner or prover treats that file as ground truth, they're acting on circular evidence the project has no way to detect or correct. This is the failure mode this rule exists to prevent — assume any "I'll just write it myself from what I remember" impulse is wrong, and use one of the three acceptable responses above instead.
304	
305	## Prover failure modes
306	
307	- **"Mathlib doesn't have it"** — the #1 failure. Do not pass it back with "try harder". Find an alternative route via the catalog (a literature/reference-fetching subagent when enabled), `WebSearch`/`WebFetch`, or `archon-informal-agent.py` for a proof-style sketch when its API credentials are configured. If the gap is in a definition, dispatch a write-capable structural subagent from your catalog. Update the chapter `.tex` with the re-routed proof before reassigning.
308	- **Wrong construction** — instruct revert (single file) or dispatch a structural subagent (cross-file). Update the chapter first.
309	- **Not using Web Search** — explicitly instruct: "use Web Search to find [arXiv ID], decompose into sub-lemmas, formalize step by step". Update the chapter with the retrieved sketch.
310	- **Early stop on a hard problem** — reject the report. Break into sub-goals in the chapter, assign L1, then L2 after L1 lands.
311	- **Tricks to bypass** (new axioms, ad-hoc weakenings) — reject. Document why this route was chosen and ensure it won't reproduce.
312	- **Repeated blockers** — same blocker over consecutive iters means rewrite the chapter or dispatch a structural subagent. Do NOT re-dispatch the same lane with cosmetic recipe variation.
313	
314	## Verification
315	
316	The loop already runs deterministic checks each iter:
317	
318	- **Sorry count** — stamped into `meta.json` (before/mid/post prover). Do not re-count by hand.
319	- **Axiom check** — runs as part of the blueprint-doctor; new axioms surface in your injected findings block.
320	- **Blueprint consistency** — `sync_leanok` resolves `\lean{...}` against the project decls; the doctor catches broken `\ref` / `\uses`.
321	
322	What's left for you: spot-check inconsistent prover self-reports; act on every entry in the injected doctor findings (or document the deferral); reject any reported completion that left a real `sorry` or introduced a new axiom.
323	
324	## Decomposition strategy
325	
326	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem: read the chapter for sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, …); read related `references/` to align with the original proof; expand the chapter if too thin (dispatch a blueprint-writing or literature-fetching subagent from the catalog, or use `WebSearch`/`WebFetch` directly); assign one sub-lemma at a time; verify, then assign the next; record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
327	
328	## Soundness check before spending budget
329	
330	**Churning and unsoundness are different signals — the critic catches the first, only you catch the second.** A recurring blocker is not always a hard-but-true gap; sometimes the target `sorry` is a statement that is simply **false as written** (a missing hypothesis, a wrong quantifier, an unstated connectedness/finiteness assumption). Pouring prover budget into a false statement burns iterations forever — the prover correctly cannot close it, the progress-critic reports CHURNING, and everyone treats it as a Mathlib gap when the real fix is one word in the statement.
331	
332	So **before committing more than one iter of prover budget to a hard or recurring `sorry`** (a target the progress-critic flags as a repeated blocker, or any target you estimate at multiple iters / >~100 LOC), first spend a cheap pass trying to **DISPROVE** the statement:
333	
334	- Instantiate it on the smallest non-trivial models — finite, degenerate, or boundary cases (e.g. for an algebra claim: `B = k × k`, `B = ℚ(√2)`, the zero ring, a one-point space). Does any satisfy the hypotheses but violate the conclusion?
335	- If you can't see it yourself, dispatch the informal / mathlib-analogist subagent from your catalog with a directive that asks specifically for a **counterexample or a satisfiability sketch**, not a proof.
336	- Check whether the source the statement claims to follow actually states it with the same hypotheses (cite-and-read discipline applies — read the local source file).
337	
338	If a counterexample turns up, the statement (or a missing hypothesis) is the bug: fix the blueprint statement, mark the Lean declaration with a `% NOTE:` for review, and do NOT assign a prover to formalize the false version. If the disproof attempt fails, you've cheaply raised your confidence that the target is true — now spend the budget. Record the disproof attempt and its outcome in the iter sidecar so the next planner doesn't repeat it.
339	
340	## Multi-agent coordination
341	
342	Provers run in parallel — one per file. Number objectives clearly; each maps to exactly one `.lean` file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside, and **list every ready sorry in that file that the prover should fill in this iter** — not just one:
343	
344	```markdown
345	## Current Objectives
346	
347	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorries in `filter_convergence` (line 156), `filter_inv` (line 188), `filter_assoc` (line 211). Blueprint: `chapters/Core.tex` (`thm:filter_convergence`, `thm:filter_inv`, `thm:filter_assoc`).
348	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `chapters/Measure.tex`.
349	3. **`ChartAlgebra.lean`** — Scaffold the file with declarations for `thm:chart_id`, `thm:chart_comp`, `thm:chart_inv` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`. Blueprint: `chapters/ChartAlgebra.tex`. (File-skeleton dispatch — see below.)
350	```
351	
352	**Agent count = file count.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per file. When a file has multiple ready sorries, list ALL of them under that file's objective — the prover handles them sequentially within one lane. Splitting a multi-sorry file across iters is artificial throttling; one lane working sequentially on three sorries finishes faster than waiting two iters for three single-sorry lanes (the prover keeps its file context warm across sorries).
353	
354	**File-skeleton dispatches.** When a blueprint chapter is complete but the corresponding `.lean` file does not yet exist (or exists but is missing declarations the chapter introduced), it is a legitimate iter objective to dispatch a prover with directive *"scaffold `Foo.lean` with declarations for `thm:a`, `thm:b`, `thm:c` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`; add the import + namespace boilerplate; do not attempt to prove anything yet"*. The next iter then fills the sorries. This is materially faster than one-iter-per-declaration scaffolding.
355	
356	**Mechanical-vs-deep partition.** Sorries split into two regimes:
357	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
358	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
359	
360	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
361	
362	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
363	
364	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
365	
366	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
367	
368	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
369	
370	## Dependency graph
371	
372	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
373	
374	```
375	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
376	```
377	
378	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.
379	
380	## Stage transitions
381	
382	Advance `PROGRESS.md` when all current-stage objectives are met:
383	
384	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (all statements formalized)
385	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)
386	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (proofs clean, compile)

113	
114	# Mathlib Analogist
115	
116	You are the read-only Mathlib-analogist subagent with **two modes**: API alignment (the default) and cross-domain inspiration. The directive's `## Mode` line selects which.
117	
118	## Mode: api-alignment
119	
120	The plan agent points you at a project declaration, a proposed definition, or a design question, and asks "what would Mathlib do, and is the project doing that?". Your job:
121	
122	1. Identify the actual design decisions at play (often multiple compressed into one question).
123	2. Find how Mathlib has resolved analogous decisions.
124	3. Compare the project's path to Mathlib's idiom.
125	4. Report whether the project should align with the idiom — and, if not, what the cost of the divergence is.
126	
127	You do **not** rubber-stamp the project's choice. If Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project skipped it (most often because the prerequisite infrastructure looked hard), say so plainly. "It works" is not enough — the cost of API fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and code that can't compose with Mathlib is real.
128	
129	## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration
130	
131	The plan agent points you at a *structural problem* — a categorical, topological, algebraic, or analytic shape that's been resisting the project's current approach — and asks "has Mathlib solved this same shape somewhere else, possibly in a completely different mathematical field?". Your job:
132	
133	1. Distill the structural problem to its categorical / algebraic / topological core, stripping away the project's specific objects.
134	2. Search *broadly* across Mathlib for that shape. Cross domains aggressively — a sheaf condition on schemes shares structure with a sheaf condition on topological spaces; a descent argument in alg geom shares structure with a descent argument in functional analysis; a fixed-point setup in measure theory shares structure with order-theoretic Knaster–Tarski.
135	3. For each analogue you find, extract the *technique* used there (the tactic combination, the categorical lemma, the unbundling trick, the inductive step).
136	4. Map the technique back to the project's setting. State concretely what porting the technique would look like.
137	
138	You are explicitly NOT checking API alignment in this mode. The goal is to surface unfamiliar territory that may be borrowable, not to validate the project's existing definitions. Distant analogues are MORE valuable than nearby ones — the planner already knows the nearby ones.
139	
140	## Scope
141	
142	**One design question per invocation.** A design question may be broad ("how should we represent the Picard scheme?") or narrow ("instance-based vs. predicate-based"). Multiple Mathlib precedents in the SAME question are welcome. What you should NOT do is sprawl across unrelated decisions in one call.

234	## Workflow
235	
236	1. **Read the directive completely.** Note the `## Mode` line first — your workflow branches on it.
237	
238	### api-alignment mode
239	
240	2. **Read the project artifact(s)** and their surrounding context — imports, neighboring declarations, the blueprint chapter the declaration corresponds to (per the `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug mapping). The blueprint often makes the mathematical intent explicit where the Lean only hints at it.
241	
242	3. **Identify the open design decision(s).** A directive that says "should this be a typeclass?" usually compresses several decisions (bundled vs unbundled, predicate vs structure, instance vs explicit). Name each.
243	
244	4. **Locate Mathlib precedents.** Use `lean_leansearch` and `lean_loogle` on the relevant names and types. Read 2–5 Mathlib files that resolve analogous decisions. For each, note:
245	   - What Mathlib chose (typeclass / structure / predicate / function).
246	   - Why (legible from the docstring, naming convention, or how the choice composes with surrounding API).
247	   - How the project's current or proposed approach compares.
248	
249	5. **Compare and judge.** For each open decision, write:
250	   - The Mathlib idiom (with citation: `Mathlib.X.Y.Foo`, line N).
251	   - The project's path (or proposed path).
252	   - The gap: identical, divergent-but-equivalent, divergent-with-cost, divergent-and-wrong.
253	   - For "divergent-with-cost" and "divergent-and-wrong": the concrete cost (bridge lemmas needed, downstream files that can't consume the project's API, parallel infrastructure that will eventually need to be unified).
254	
255	6. **Render a verdict per decision.** One of:
256	   - **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — the project should use the idiom; if shipped code diverges, refactor.
257	   - **DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY** — divergence is the right call for this project; document why in `analogies/<slug>.md`.
258	   - **PROCEED** — no Mathlib precedent applies; project's path is reasonable.
259	   - **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL** — Mathlib doesn't have the idiom yet; the project must build it (this is the case for genuinely new infrastructure).
260	
261	7. **Write the persistent analogy file** to `analogies/<slug>.md`. Format below.
262	
263	8. **Write the report** to `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md`.
264	
265	### cross-domain-inspiration mode
266	
267	2. **Re-state the structural problem in your own words** as the most abstract version you can defend. Strip the project's objects. Aim for a one-paragraph re-statement that names only the categorical / topological / algebraic primitives.
268	
269	3. **Brainstorm analogues across Mathlib.** Before searching, list 5–10 mathematical situations *in other Mathlib domains* that share the same structural shape. Be aggressive about distance — if the problem is sheaf-shaped, look at probability sheaves AND topological sheaves AND order-theoretic sheaves. If it's a fixed-point setup, look at Knaster–Tarski (order theory), Banach (functional analysis), Schauder (topology), Kleene (lattice theory), and so on.
270	
271	4. **Verify analogues with the LSP.** For each candidate, use `lean_leansearch` and `lean_loogle` to confirm the construction actually lives in Mathlib. Read the Mathlib file. Discard candidates that turn out to be only superficially similar.
272	
273	5. **Extract the technique.** For each verified analogue, write:
274	   - The Mathlib citation (`Mathlib.X.Y.Foo`, line N).
275	   - The structural problem solved there, in the same abstract vocabulary you used in step 2.
276	   - The technique: the tactic combination, the key lemma, the categorical reformulation, the unbundling step — whatever the actual proof or construction depends on.
277	   - The mapping back: how would this technique translate to the project's setting? Be concrete — name the project's objects, point to the corresponding Mathlib constructs.
278	   - The cost of porting: typeclass scaffolding needed, lemmas to be ported, anything the Mathlib version assumes that the project doesn't have.
279	
280	6. **Skip analogues whose technique matches a "Failed approaches" bullet.** If the analogue's technique reduces to something the directive already tried, drop it.
281	
282	7. **Render a verdict per analogue.** One of:
283	   - **ANALOGUE_FOUND** — a Mathlib precedent with a portable technique. The planner should consider trying it next iter.
284	   - **PARTIAL_ANALOGUE** — Mathlib solves a related but not identical shape; the technique is suggestive but not directly portable. Worth a deeper look only if the higher-priority options fail.
285	   - **NO_USEFUL_ANALOGUE** — Mathlib has the shape but the technique doesn't generalize beyond Mathlib's specific setting.
286	
287	8. **Rank.** Order ANALOGUE_FOUND verdicts by porting cost (lowest first). The planner reads top-to-bottom.
288	
289	9. **Write the persistent file** to `analogies/<slug>.md` and the report to `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md`. Format below — the cross-domain section is separate from the api-alignment section.

372	## Report format
373	
374	The report's body depends on the mode.
375	
376	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Mode`, `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, plus the mode-specific question/problem restatement, the verdicts/analogues table, and the overall verdict. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty** — do NOT pad sections with "(none)" / "N/A" content.
377	
378	In api-alignment mode:
379	
380	- `## Must-fix-this-iter`: omit when no ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB verdict applies to shipped code.
381	- `## Major`: omit when no ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB verdict applies to in-proposal code.
382	- `## Informational`: omit when there's nothing worth surfacing beyond what's already in the verdicts table.
383	
384	In cross-domain-inspiration mode:
385	
386	- `## Top suggestion`: required only when at least one ANALOGUE_FOUND verdict exists. When every analogue was NO_USEFUL_ANALOGUE, replace with a one-sentence "no analogues with portable techniques surfaced" line under the verdicts table; do not invent a top suggestion to fill the slot.
387	- `## Discarded`: omit when no analogues were considered-and-rejected.
388	
389	In both modes: stop searching once you have a confident finding. Don't enumerate near-misses exhaustively to look thorough — three solid analogues outrank a list of fifteen with one-line "doesn't quite work" notes.

493	- **Don't rubber-stamp.** When Mathlib has the idiom, say so clearly. "Works" is not enough.
494	- **Cite, don't allude.** Every Mathlib reference must be a real path + line. Use the LSP tools to verify.
495	- **Severity is strict.** ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB on shipped code is must-fix-this-iter — the cost compounds with every iter the divergence persists.
496	- **Cost is concrete.** "Bridge lemmas" / "parallel API needing N translations" / "downstream files blocked" — name the specific cost, not a generic "fragmentation".
497	- **In cross-domain mode, distance is value.** A category-theoretic analogue of an algebraic-geometry problem is more useful than another algebraic-geometry analogue, because the planner already knows the nearby ones. Aggressively cross domains.
498	- **Don't suggest porting a failed approach.** The directive's "Failed approaches" tells you what NOT to recommend. If your favorite analogue's technique reduces to something already tried, drop it.

16	(arithmetic genus; protected — cannot be re-typed).

28	## Routes
29	
30	The witness OBJECT for positive genus is built by **Route A (Picard scheme via
31	FGA)** — mandatory and essentially unavoidable. The genus-0 arm is a separate,
32	lower-risk rigidity statement (object trivial); its proof route is under
33	determination, with two fallbacks held live.

35	### Route A (COMMITTED) — Picard scheme / Albanese via FGA
36	
37	Hilbert/Quot representability + identity-component `Pic⁰` + Albanese universal
38	property. Decomposition A.1–A.4 in `Jacobian.tex` § "Route A". Literature in-tree:
39	`references/kleiman-picard.pdf` (§4 existence, §5 `Pic⁰`), `references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.pdf`
40	(Quot/Hilbert engine).
41	
42	**Positive-genus arm:** `J := Pic⁰_{C/k}`, a dim-`g` abelian variety; the Albanese
43	UP gives `isAlbaneseFor`. This is the project's critical path: the object must be a
44	real dim-`g` abelian variety even when `C(k)=∅`, so the FGA engine is required
45	unconditionally.

47	### Genus-0 rigidity — the keystone `rigidity_over_kbar`, decoupled
48	
49	The genus-0 witness OBJECT is the trivial `J = Spec k` (skeleton already 6/7
50	closed); it needs **no** Picard scheme. What remains is a single rigidity
51	STATEMENT: over `k̄`, a genus-0 curve is `≅ ℙ¹`, and any pointed `f : C → A` killing
52	`P` is constant. Three candidate proof routes, ranked:
53	
54	- **(c) [COMMITTED] targeted char-free AV-rigidity lemma.** "Every morphism from a
55	  genus-0 curve (`≅ ℙ¹` over `k̄`) to an abelian variety is constant." **Minimal chain**
56	  (sources now in tree — Mumford + Hartshorne + Milne): (i) **Rigidity Lemma** (Mumford
57	  *Abelian Varieties*, Form I, §4 p.43; = Milne Thm 1.1) — `V` complete, `V×W→Z` trivial
58	  on `V×{w0}` ⟹ factors through `W`; this is **CUBE-FREE** (a properness/closedness
59	  argument, NOT the theorem of the cube). (ii) `ℙ¹ → A` constant (Milne Prop 3.10's
60	  packaged corollary; Mumford leaves it as a consequence of the rigidity lemma). (iii)
61	  genus-0 + `k̄`-point ⟹ `≅ ℙ¹` (Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 / Ex IV.1.3, Riemann–Roch-flavoured).
62	  Uses NEITHER Serre duality NOR Picard representability. **Decisive reason (c) beats the
63	  cheap-ℙ¹ `df=0` route:** over `k̄`, `H^0(ℙ¹,Ω)=H^0(ℙ¹,O(−2))=0` is *elementary* (the
64	  iter-155 chart-by-chart refutation does NOT touch the concrete `ℙ¹`), so `df=0` is reachable
65	  — but `df=0 ⟹ constant` is **FALSE in char `p`** without Frobenius descent. The protected
66	  goal is arbitrary `[Field k]`, so char-freeness is decisive. **Cube question RESOLVED
67	  iter-157** (NEGATIVE): the split is opposite the iter-156 hope — the multi-factor induction
68	  (Cor 1.5) is cube-free, but the *base case* "`ℙ¹→A` constant" (an AV has no rational curves)
69	  rests irreducibly on the cube. So `rigidity_lemma` is cube-free + prover-ready but does NOT
70	  alone close the arm; the cube is the heavy keystone, SHARED with Route A's Albanese UP (not
71	  throwaway). (c) stays cheaper than (b) (= cube AND representability). Decoupled from FGA.
72	- **(b) byproduct of the engine.** Once Route A exists, `Alb(genus-0)=Pic⁰(ℙ¹)=0`,

83	theorem snd_left_isClosedMap
84	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper X.hom] :
85	    IsClosedMap (snd X Y).left.base := by

92	
93	/-- **The dense-open agreement (the genuine geometric content, deferred).** Mumford's open
94	`X × V` together with the slice-constancy `f(x, y) = f(x₀, y)` on it, packaged as the single
95	existential that `rigidity_core`'s gluing step consumes: there is a non-empty open `U` of
96	`(X ⊗ Y).left` on which `f` and the collapsed map `retract ≫ f` agree as scheme morphisms.
97	
98	This lemma carries the **collapse hypothesis** `_hf : f(X × {y₀}) = {z₀}` (encoded
99	`lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀`), which is exactly what makes Mumford's open
100	`V := Y ∖ G` non-empty (`y₀ ∉ G`, since the rigidified slice `f(X × {y₀}) = {z₀} ⊆ U` lands in
101	the affine `U`, so its image under `snd` avoids `G = snd '' (f ⁻¹ F)`). Without `_hf` the lemma
102	is **false** (e.g. `f := fst : X ⊗ Y ⟶ X = Z` has no open of agreement). The full instance set
103	(`GeometricallyIrreducible`, `IsReduced`, `IsSeparated`) and `_hf` only strengthen the
104	antecedent.

106	This is where the two char-free Mathlib bridges of `rigidity_core`'s docstring are discharged:
107	the closed-map argument (`IsProper.toUniversallyClosed` ⟹ the projection is closed) produces the
108	non-empty open `U = X × V`, and the affine-constancy argument
109	(`isField_of_universallyClosed` on each proper integral slice mapping to an affine) supplies the
110	scheme-level equality on `U`. It is the sole remaining `sorry` of the Rigidity-Lemma chain. -/
111	theorem rigidity_eqOn_dense_open
112	    {X Y Z : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
113	    [IsProper X.hom]
114	    [GeometricallyIrreducible (X ⊗ Y).hom]
115	    [IsReduced (X ⊗ Y).left]
116	    [IsSeparated Z.hom]
117	    (f : (X ⊗ Y) ⟶ Z)
118	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X)
119	    (y₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Y)
120	    (z₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Z)
121	    (_hf : lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀) :
122	    ∃ U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens, (U : Set (X ⊗ Y).left).Nonempty ∧
123	      (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) ≫ f.left =
124	        (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) ≫
125	          (lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left := by
126	  -- Mumford's construction of the open `U = X × V`, `V = Y - G`, `G = p₂(f⁻¹(Z - U₀))`.
127	  -- Bridge 1 (`snd_left_isClosedMap`) makes `G` closed; the collapse hypothesis `_hf` makes
128	  -- `y₀ ∉ G` so `V` (hence `U`) is non-empty; bridge 2 (affine-constancy) is the agreement.
129	  have hclosed : IsClosedMap (snd X Y).left.base := snd_left_isClosedMap
130	  -- `Spec k̄` is a single point; transport the instance to the monoidal unit's underlying scheme.
131	  haveI hsub : Subsingleton (↥(𝟙_ (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)))).left) :=
132	    inferInstanceAs (Subsingleton (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)))
133	  -- The chosen `k̄`-point of `Spec k̄` (`= (𝟙_).left`).
134	  have ptk : (𝟙_ (Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)))).left :=
135	    (inferInstance : Inhabited (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar))).default
136	  -- The image point `z₀` in `Z` and an affine open neighbourhood `U₀ ∋ z₀`.
137	  let z₀pt : Z.left := z₀.left.base ptk
138	  obtain ⟨U₀, _hU₀aff, hz₀U₀, -⟩ := exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := Z.left)
139	    (x := z₀pt) (U := ⊤) trivial
140	  -- `G = p₂(f⁻¹(Z - U₀))` is closed (image of a closed set under the closed map `p₂`).
141	  set Gset := (snd X Y).left.base '' (f.left.base ⁻¹' (U₀ : Set Z.left)ᶜ) with hGdef
142	  have hG : IsClosed Gset := hclosed _ (U₀.isOpen.isClosed_compl.preimage f.left.base.hom.2)
143	  -- `U = p₂⁻¹(Y - G)` (Mumford's `X × V`) is open.
144	  have hUopen : IsOpen ((snd X Y).left.base ⁻¹' Gsetᶜ) :=
145	    (hG.isOpen_compl).preimage (snd X Y).left.base.hom.2
146	  -- The slice section `s : X → X ⊗ Y`, `x ↦ (x, y₀)`, and the points `y₀`, `x₀`.
147	  let s := (lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀)).left
148	  let y₀pt : Y.left := y₀.left.base ptk
149	  let x₀pt : X.left := x₀.left.base ptk
150	  -- GAP (pullback-fibre topology, isolated): every point of the slice `p₂⁻¹{y₀}` lies in the
151	  -- image of the section `s`. True because `y₀` is a `k̄`-point: the scheme fibre over it is
152	  -- `X ×_{Spec k̄} Spec k̄ = X`, and `s` is the canonical identification of `X` with that fibre.
153	  -- (Needs the range-of-pullback-projection-fibre lemma; see task_results for the precise gap.)
154	  have hfib : (snd X Y).left.base ⁻¹' {y₀pt} ⊆ Set.range s.base := sorry
155	  -- `y₀ ∉ G`: any point over `y₀` is `s x`, and `_hf` collapses `f (s x) = z₀ ∈ U₀`.
156	  have hy₀ : y₀pt ∉ Gset := by
157	    rintro ⟨q, hq, hsndq⟩
158	    obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := hfib (by simpa using hsndq)
159	    apply hq
160	    have hcomp : s ≫ f.left = (toUnit X ≫ z₀).left := by
161	      rw [← Over.comp_left]; exact congrArg Over.Hom.left _hf
162	    have hfx : f.left.base (s.base x) = z₀pt := by
163	      rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_apply, hcomp, Over.comp_left, Scheme.Hom.comp_apply]
164	      change z₀.left.base ((toUnit X).left.base x) = z₀.left.base ptk
165	      congr 1; exact Subsingleton.elim _ _
166	    rw [hfx]; exact hz₀U₀
167	  -- Assemble `U`, witness its non-emptiness by `s x₀` (which lies over `y₀ ∈ V`).
168	  refine ⟨⟨_, hUopen⟩, ⟨s.base x₀pt, ?_⟩, ?_⟩
169	  · change (snd X Y).left.base (s.base x₀pt) ∈ Gsetᶜ
170	    have hsnd : (snd X Y).left.base (s.base x₀pt) = y₀pt := by
171	      have hcomp : s ≫ (snd X Y).left = (toUnit X ≫ y₀).left := by
172	        rw [← Over.comp_left]; exact congrArg Over.Hom.left (lift_snd (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀))
173	      rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_apply, hcomp, Over.comp_left, Scheme.Hom.comp_apply]
174	      change y₀.left.base ((toUnit X).left.base x₀pt) = y₀.left.base ptk
175	      congr 1; exact Subsingleton.elim _ _
176	    rw [Set.mem_compl_iff, hsnd]; exact hy₀
177	  · -- Bridge 2 (affine-constancy): on `U = X × V` each proper slice `X × {y}` maps into the
178	    -- affine `U₀`, hence to a single point `f(x₀, y)`, so `f` and `retract ≫ f` agree on `U`.
179	    -- This is the scheme-morphism equality that needs the relative "proper-into-affine is
180	    -- constant" / `O_X = k̄`-pushforward bridge; isolated as the residual geometric input.
181	    sorry
182	
183	/-- **Geometric core of the Rigidity Lemma (PROVEN, modulo `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`).** With
184	`X` complete (proper) and `x₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ X` a `k̄`-point, the morphism `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` is invariant
185	under the "collapse-the-`X`-axis-onto-`x₀`" endomorphism
186	`retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`:
187	
188	  `f = retract ≫ f`,
189	
190	i.e. `f(x, y) = f(x₀, y)` for all `(x, y)` — `f` depends only on the `Y`-coordinate. This is
191	the entirety of the *geometric* content of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma (Form I); the rest of
192	`rigidity_lemma` is the cartesian-monoidal algebra discharged by `rigidity_snd_lift`.
193	
194	This core is now **proven** by the project's scheme-level rigidity (replicated inline from
195	Mathlib's `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`, since the wrapper `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen`
196	lives in the downstream `AlgebraicJacobian.Rigidity`): two maps out of the geometrically
197	irreducible reduced `X ⊗ Y` into the separated `Z` that agree on a non-empty open agree
198	everywhere. The single deferred input is the existence of that non-empty open together with the
199	agreement on it — `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` — which is the actual geometry below.
200	
201	This core carries the **collapse hypothesis** `_hf : f(X × {y₀}) = {z₀}` (encoded
202	`lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀`), passed straight through to
203	`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`: it is exactly what makes Mumford's open `V := Y ∖ G` non-empty
204	(`y₀ ∉ G`). Without it the core statement is **false** (`f := fst` is not collapse-invariant).
205	
206	## Mumford's proof of `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` (Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43)
207	
208	Set `g(y) = f(x₀, y)` (so `retract ≫ f` is exactly `(x, y) ↦ g(y)`). The open `V` is produced as
209	follows: let `U` be an affine open neighbourhood of a chosen point in `Z`, `F = Z ∖ U`, and
210	`G = (snd X Y) '' (f ⁻¹ F)`. Then for each `y ∈ V := Y ∖ G`, the slice `f(X × {y}) ⊆ U`, and `f`
211	agrees with `retract ≫ f` on the non-empty open `X × V`.
212	
213	This rests on **two Mathlib bridges still to be built** (the cube-free heart):
214	
215	  1. **Properness ⇒ the projection is a closed map.** `IsProper X.hom` is universally closed
216	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper.toUniversallyClosed`), and `snd X Y` is the base change of
217	     `X.hom` along `Y.hom`, hence universally closed, hence a closed map
218	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.UniversallyClosed.universally_isClosedMap` instantiated at the relevant
219	     pullback square). So `G = snd '' (closed)` is closed and `V` is open. The missing glue is
220	     identifying the monoidal `snd X Y` in `Over (Spec k̄)` with the scheme-theoretic pullback
221	     projection `Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` and transporting `IsClosedMap` across that
222	     identification. (Search: `MorphismProperty.pullback`, `Over.tensor`/`Over.cartesian…`,
223	     `Limits.pullback.snd`.)
224	
225	  2. **A proper connected variety mapping to an affine has image a single point.** For `y ∈ V`,
226	     the proper connected slice `X × {y}` maps under `f` into the affine `U`; a global regular
227	     function on a proper integral `k̄`-scheme is constant. Mathlib *has* the key fact:
228	     `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed` — for `X` integral and `f : X ⟶ Spec K`
229	     universally closed (`K` a field), `Γ(X, ⊤)` is a field. Combined with `Γ(affine U) → Γ(X×{y})`
230	     being a `k̄`-algebra map into a field that is finite over `k̄`
231	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` under `LocallyOfFiniteType`) and
232	     `k̄` algebraically closed, the map `X × {y} → U` factors through a single point. This is the
233	     "global-sections-constant" argument; assembling it into "image is one point" is the
234	     remaining work.
235	
236	These are exactly the "two Mathlib bridges to find/build" flagged in `PROGRESS.md`. iter-157
237	located concrete Mathlib entry points for *both* (named above), upgrading the prior "likely
238	lacks this" assessment: the obstruction is assembly + the monoidal-`snd`-as-pullback
239	identification, not absent infrastructure. Both bridges are char-free (no theorem of the cube,
240	no cohomology beyond `H⁰`). Until they are assembled, `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` is the sole
241	`sorry` of the Rigidity-Lemma chain — both the categorical reduction (`rigidity_lemma`,
242	`rigidity_snd_lift`) and the scheme-level gluing (`rigidity_core`) are closed. -/
243	theorem rigidity_core
244	    {X Y Z : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
245	    [IsProper X.hom]
246	    [GeometricallyIrreducible (X ⊗ Y).hom]
247	    [IsReduced (X ⊗ Y).left]
248	    [IsSeparated Z.hom]
249	    (f : (X ⊗ Y) ⟶ Z)
250	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X)
251	    (y₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Y)
252	    (z₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ Z)
253	    (_hf : lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀) :
254	    f = lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f := by
255	  -- The gluing step is scheme-level rigidity: two maps out of a geometrically irreducible reduced
256	  -- source into a separated target that agree on a non-empty open agree everywhere. We replicate
257	  -- the argument of `AlgebraicJacobian.Rigidity`'s `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` inline (that
258	  -- file is *downstream* of this one, so its wrapper is unavailable here) directly from Mathlib's
259	  -- `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`. The non-empty open and the agreement on it are the
260	  -- genuine geometric content, isolated in `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`.
261	  obtain ⟨U, hU, h⟩ := rigidity_eqOn_dense_open f x₀ y₀ z₀ _hf
262	  -- `Spec k̄` is a single point, so geometric irreducibility makes `(X ⊗ Y).left` irreducible.
263	  haveI : IrreducibleSpace (X ⊗ Y).left :=
264	    GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton (X ⊗ Y).hom
265	  -- A non-empty open of an irreducible space is dense, so its inclusion is dominant.
266	  haveI : IsDominant (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) :=
267	    Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant_ι (IsOpen.dense U.isOpen hU)
268	  -- Provide separatedness of `Z.left` over `Spec k̄` in the `OverClass.fromOver` form.
269	  haveI : IsSeparated (Z.left ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)) := ‹IsSeparated Z.hom›
270	  -- Promote the underlying-scheme equality to an `Over (Spec k̄)` equality.
271	  refine Over.OverMorphism.ext ?_
272	  exact ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated' (S := Spec (.of kbar))
273	    (X := (X ⊗ Y).left) (Y := Z.left) (f := f.left)
274	    (g := (lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left) U.ι h
275	
276	/-- **Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I).** Let `X` be a complete (proper) variety and `Y`, `Z`
277	any varieties over `k̄`. If `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` collapses the slice `X × {y₀}` to a single point
278	`z₀` (encoded as `lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀`), then `f` factors through
279	the second projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y`, i.e. there is `g : Y ⟶ Z` with `f = snd ≫ g`.
280	
281	This is the cube-free, cohomology-free entry point of the chain: its only inputs are that
282	completeness of `X` makes the projection a closed map, and that a proper connected variety has
283	no nonconstant map to an affine variety. Valid in arbitrary characteristic.
284	
285	SCAFFOLD: signature refined by the iter-157 prover; see blueprint `thm:rigidity_lemma`
286	(Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch. II §4, p. 43).
287	
288	## iter-157 signature correction (the scaffold was false as stated)
289
Now the two analogist directives — one per residual bridge.

# Mathlib Analogist Directive

## Mode
api-alignment

## Slug
rigidity-hfib

## Design question
What is the Mathlib idiom for describing the **fibre of a pullback projection
`Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` over a `k̄`-rational point of `Y`** — specifically, is there
an existing result (or a clean idiomatic assembly) showing that this fibre is exactly the image
of the canonical section `s : X → X ×_{Spec k̄} Y` induced by the rational point? Concretely we
need to close, for `X Y : Over (Spec k̄)` with `kbar` algebraically closed:

```
hfib : (snd X Y).left.base ⁻¹' {y₀pt} ⊆ Set.range s.base
```

where `y₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ Y` is a morphism over `Spec k̄` (so `y₀.left` is a SECTION of `Y.hom`, making
`y₀pt := y₀.left.base ptk` a `k̄`-rational point with residue field `k̄`), and
`s := (lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀)).left : X.left ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left` is the slice section
`x ↦ (x, y₀)`. (Equality `p₂⁻¹{y₀pt} = range s` in fact holds; we only need `⊆`.)

## Project artifact(s) under question
- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:154` — the `hfib` sorry inside
  `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`.
- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:111-181` — the enclosing lemma; `s`, `y₀pt`,
  `ptk`, `snd X Y` are defined at L131-149. Note `(snd X Y).left = Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom`
  via `CategoryTheory.Over.snd_left` (an exact rewrite; the monoidal `⊗` on `Over S` is the
  chosen pullback).

## Why now
The iter-158 prover lane reduced the entire non-emptiness of Mumford's open `V := Y∖G` to exactly
this fibre fact, genuinely consuming the collapse hypothesis `_hf`. The prover located the
pullback point-set machinery (`Scheme.Pullback.carrierEquiv`, `Scheme.Pullback.Triplet` /
`.tensor` / `.ext_iff` / `.carrierEquiv_eq_iff`, `Scheme.Pullback.exists_preimage_of_isPullback`,
`Scheme.Hom.fiber` in `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Fiber`) but found **no off-the-shelf lemma** that
the fibre of `pullback.snd` over a rational point is the source. The progress-critic bound a
fallback: a scoped analogist consult on this bridge BEFORE another prover round. I am about to
either (a) blueprint a precise buildable sub-lemma `fibre_pullback_snd_over_rational_point` and
hand it to a prover, or (b) if you find an existing Mathlib result, point the prover straight at it.

## Hints (optional)
The intended math: by `Scheme.Pullback.carrierEquiv`, a point of `X ×_{Spec k̄} Y` over `y₀pt` is
a `Triplet (x, y₀pt, pt∈Spec k̄)` plus a point of `Spec(κ(x) ⊗_{κ(s)} κ(y₀pt))`. Since
`κ(s) = κ(y₀pt) = k̄` (the latter because `y₀.left` is a section of `Y.hom`), the tensor is
`κ(x) ⊗_{k̄} k̄ ≅ κ(x)`, a field, so `Spec` of it is a single point — the fibre point is determined
by its `X`-coordinate `x`, and that point is `s x`. The three sub-questions:
(a) does Mathlib give `κ(y₀pt) = k̄` (residue field at a rational point / image of a section over
the base field) idiomatically? (b) `Subsingleton (Spec (κ(x) ⊗_{k̄} k̄))` from "tensoring a field
with the base field over itself stays a field/single point" — what's the idiom? (c) feeding that
through `carrierEquiv` to land `= s x`. Also: does Mathlib have any "base change of a scheme along
a field extension; fibre over a rational point = source" lemma in the `Scheme.Pullback` /
`Scheme.Hom.fiber` / `AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback` area, perhaps phrased via `Spec.map` of a
residue-field inclusion? Cross-check `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback`, `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Fiber`,
`AlgebraicGeometry.residueField`.

## Severity expectation
high-stakes

# Mathlib Analogist Directive

## Mode
api-alignment

## Slug
rigidity-affineconst

## Design question
Does Mathlib have an idiom for the **relative** statement "a proper morphism with
geometrically-connected fibres into an affine target is constant along fibres", expressed as a
**scheme-morphism equality**, that we can use to close the agreement equation of the Rigidity
Lemma? Concretely we must prove, on the open `U = X × V ⊆ (X ⊗ Y).left`:

```
U.ι ≫ f.left = U.ι ≫ (lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left
```

i.e. on `U` the map `f` agrees with "collapse the `X`-axis to `x₀` then apply `f`". The
mathematical content: for each `y ∈ V`, the proper connected slice `X × {y}` maps under `f` into
the affine `U₀ ∋ z₀`, hence to a SINGLE point `f(x₀, y)`; so `f` factors through the second
projection on `U`. This is Stein-factorization / "proper-into-affine is constant on fibres"
content, but needed as a morphism equality, not just a set-theoretic image statement.

## Project artifact(s) under question
- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:181` — the agreement-equation sorry (bridge 2)
  inside `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`.
- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:111-181` — enclosing lemma; `U₀` is an affine
  open nbhd of `z₀pt`, `V = Y∖G`, `U = p₂⁻¹V`, `s` the slice section; all set up by L138-176.
- Located ABSOLUTE facts (not yet assembled into the relative equality):
  `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed` (`Γ(X,⊤)` is a field for `X` integral
  universally closed over a field `K`), `AlgebraicGeometry.finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`
  (under `LocallyOfFiniteType`).

## Why now
This is described by the iter-158 prover as "the hardest residual input of the chain" and is
SHARED with Route A's Albanese universal property (Milne §III.6), so it is not throwaway. The
progress-critic bound a fallback: scoped analogist consult on this bridge BEFORE another prover
round. The absolute fact (`Γ(proper integral /field) is a field`) is in Mathlib; the open question
is whether Mathlib has the **relative assembly** — proper pushforward `f_* O_X`, Stein
factorization, or a "morphism to affine determined by global sections / factors through
`Spec Γ`" packaging — that turns "each slice's image is a point" into the morphism equality on `U`.

## Hints (optional)
Search targets: Stein factorization (`AlgebraicGeometry.SteinFactorization`? likely absent),
proper pushforward of the structure sheaf, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Γ` adjunction
(`AlgebraicGeometry.ΓSpec` / `Scheme.toSpecΓ` / `Γ`-`Spec` adjunction: a morphism `W ⟶ Spec R`
corresponds to a ring map `R ⟶ Γ(W)`), `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.homEquiv` for maps INTO an
affine scheme (a morphism `W ⟶ Spec R` is determined by `R ⟶ Γ(W,⊤)`). The likely idiomatic route:
maps into the affine `U₀` are determined by their ring map on global sections; if `Γ` of each
slice is `k̄`, the ring map is constant per slice, forcing factorization through `p₂`. Also check
whether `AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineHom` / `affineTargetClass` or the morphism-into-affine
`Scheme.ΓSpecIso`/`Spec`-counit gives the "determined by global sections" handle. Is there a
relative `proper + O-connected ⟹ f_* O = O_base` (the algebraic-geometry form of
"proper-connected-into-affine is constant")? Distinguish: do we genuinely need `f_*` cohomology
(absent), or can the GLOBAL-SECTIONS adjunction + per-slice field argument suffice without it?
This distinction is the key thing I need from you — whether bridge 2 is a 1–2 iter idiomatic
assembly or a multi-iter Mathlib gap-fill.

## Severity expectation
high-stakes

17	`hfib : (snd X Y).left.base ⁻¹' {y₀pt} ⊆ Set.range s.base`.

19	## Verdicts (summary)
20	
21	| Decision | Verdict | Severity |
22	|---|---|---|
23	| Which machinery proves the fibre-over-rational-point fact (Triplet/tensor/carrierEquiv vs. `IsPullback` + `image_preimage_eq_of_isPullback`) | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | major (proposal-stage) |
24	| How the "y₀ is a section/rational point" hypothesis enters (residue field `κ(y₀pt)=k̄` vs. `Over.w` + `tensorUnit_hom`) | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | major (proposal-stage) |
25	
26	**There is no single off-the-shelf lemma** "fibre of `pullback.snd` over a rational point = range
27	of the section" (confirmed: `loogle` on `IsPullback … Set.range` returns nothing; `Scheme.Hom.fiber`
28	in `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Fiber.lean` only gives `range_fiberι : range (fiberι) = f ⁻¹' {y}`,
29	which re-introduces the residue field of `y₀pt`). But there **is** a short, fully idiomatic
30	assembly that bypasses the entire tensor/residue-field computation.
31	
32	## Major
33	
34	Both ALIGN verdicts apply to a proof the prover is *about to write* (not shipped), so the planner
35	can simply adopt the idiom rather than refactor. The concern is that the iter-158 lane located the
36	`Triplet`/`tensor`/`carrierEquiv` API and was about to commit to it — that is the heavyweight
37	parallel route and should be abandoned **before** any prover round on those sub-lemmas.
38	
39	- **The fibre fact**: use the `IsPullback`-level range lemma
40	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image_preimage_eq_of_isPullback`
41	  (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/PullbackCarrier.lean:414`):
42	  `(h : IsPullback fst snd f g)(s : Set X) : snd.base '' (fst.base ⁻¹' s) = g.base ⁻¹' (f.base '' s)`.
43	  Feed it a single pulled-back square for the slice section, built by the standard pasting
44	  calculus. The `Triplet`/`tensor`/`carrierEquiv` route would instead force three sub-builds —
45	  `κ(y₀pt) = k̄`, `Subsingleton (Spec (κ(x) ⊗_{k̄} k̄))`, and `carrierEquiv` plumbing — two of which
46	  are residue-field / commutative-algebra infrastructure with no off-the-shelf form. **All three
47	  are unnecessary.**
48	
49	- **The section hypothesis**: `y₀.left ≫ Y.hom = 𝟙 S` is `rfl`-clean via
50	  `CategoryTheory.Over.w y₀` + `CategoryTheory.Over.tensorUnit_hom`
51	  (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Cartesian/Over.lean:68`, `:= rfl`). This is what makes the
52	  outer pasting square a pullback; the residue-field reading "`κ(y₀pt)=k̄`" is never needed.
53	
54	### Concrete proof skeleton (LSP-verified lemma names, iter-159 Mathlib)
55	
56	With `p₁ := pullback.fst X.hom Y.hom`, `p₂ := pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom`
57	(`(snd X Y).left = p₂` by `Over.snd_left`; `s : X.left ⟶ pullback X.hom Y.hom`):
58	
59	1. `hsp1 : s ≫ p₁ = 𝟙 X.left` — `lift_fst` ▸ `← Over.fst_left` / `← Over.comp_left`.
60	2. `hsp2 : s ≫ p₂ = X.hom ≫ y₀.left` — `lift_snd` ▸ `← Over.snd_left` / `← Over.comp_left`, plus
61	   `(toUnit X).left = X.hom` (`Over.w (toUnit X)` + `tensorUnit_hom` + `comp_id`).
62	3. `hsec : y₀.left ≫ Y.hom = 𝟙 (Spec (.of kbar))` — `Over.w y₀` then `tensorUnit_hom`.
63	4. `houter : IsPullback (s ≫ p₁) X.hom X.hom (y₀.left ≫ Y.hom)` —
64	   `rw [hsp1, hsec]; exact IsPullback.of_horiz_isIso ⟨by simp⟩`.
65	5. `hL : IsPullback s X.hom p₂ y₀.left` —
66	   `IsPullback.of_right houter hsp2 (IsPullback.of_hasPullback X.hom Y.hom)`.
67	6. `hrange : Set.range s.base = p₂.base ⁻¹' Set.range y₀.left.base` —
68	   `simpa [Set.image_univ, Set.preimage_univ] using
69	     Scheme.Pullback.image_preimage_eq_of_isPullback hL.flip Set.univ`.
70	7. `rw [Over.snd_left, hrange]; exact Set.preimage_mono (Set.singleton_subset_iff.mpr ⟨ptk, rfl⟩)`.
71	
72	Pasting lemmas used (all `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Limits/Shapes/Pullback/IsPullback/Basic.lean`,
73	loogle-verified):
74	`IsPullback.of_right`, `IsPullback.of_horiz_isIso`, `IsPullback.flip`,
75	`IsPullback.of_hasPullback`. The orientation of step 5: `of_right` recovers the left square
76	`IsPullback s X.hom p₂ y₀.left` from the *outer* identity square (step 4, both legs `𝟙` hence
77	`IsIso`) and the canonical pullback square `IsPullback p₁ p₂ X.hom Y.hom`; its side condition is
78	exactly `hsp2`. Step 6 needs `.flip` so that the `image '' (preimage)` reads off `s` (not `X.hom`).
79	
80	Step 6 in fact yields the **equality** `range s.base = p₂⁻¹(range y₀.left)`; with the in-scope
81	`Subsingleton` instance `hsub` (L131) one gets `range y₀.left = {y₀pt}` and hence
82	`p₂⁻¹{y₀pt} = range s.base` exactly. The required `⊆` needs only `y₀pt ∈ range y₀.left` (step 7),
83	so the subsingleton is not even required. **Char-free; no `[IsAlgClosed]`; no residue fields; no
84	tensor products** — matching the fact that the project's `hfib` carries no `[IsAlgClosed]`.
85	
86	## Informational
87	
88	- The `Triplet`/`tensor`/`carrierEquiv` API (`PullbackCarrier.lean:41-291`) is not "wrong" — it is
89	  the *fine* point-set description of a pullback (a point = a triplet `(x,y,s)` + a prime of
90	  `κ(x)⊗_{κ(s)}κ(y)`). It is simply the wrong granularity for a "where is the fibre" question.
91	  `image_preimage_eq_of_isPullback` / `exists_preimage_of_isPullback` (the *coarse* topological
92	  layer of the same file) are the intended tools here.
93	- `range_snd : Set.range (pullback.snd f g) = g ⁻¹' Set.range f` (`PullbackCarrier.lean:331`) is the
94	  same fact specialized to the *chosen* `Limits.pullback`; one could alternatively transport the
95	  iso `X.left ≅ pullback y₀.left p₂` and apply `range_snd`, but `image_preimage_eq_of_isPullback`
96	  on `hL.flip` is the direct `IsPullback`-level route and avoids the iso transport.
97	
98	## Persistent file
99	- `analogies/rigidity-hfib.md` — design-rationale + full skeleton captured for future iters.
100	
101	Overall verdict: no off-the-shelf lemma exists, but the project should ALIGN with the
102	`IsPullback` + `image_preimage_eq_of_isPullback` idiom (≈10-line char-free assembly) and abandon
103	the located `Triplet`/`tensor`/`carrierEquiv`+residue-field route, which would require three
104	unnecessary sub-builds.
105

The `hfib` bridge is solved: a ~10-line char-free assembly via `image_preimage_eq_of_isPullback`, abandoning the heavyweight `Triplet`/tensor route. Concrete LSP-verified skeleton in hand. Now the deep bridge 2 report.

1	# Mathlib Analogist Report
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	rigidity-affineconst
8	
9	## Iteration
10	159
11	
12	## Question
13	Does Mathlib have an idiom for the **relative** statement "a proper morphism with
14	geometrically-connected fibres into an affine target is constant along fibres", expressed as a
15	**scheme-morphism equality**, usable to close the agreement equation (bridge 2) of
16	`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` (`AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:181`)?
17	
18	## Verdicts (summary)
19	
20	| Decision | Verdict | Severity |
21	|---|---|---|
22	| 1. Relative Stein / proper pushforward `f_* O = O` framing | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL | informational (do not pursue) |
23	| 2. Global-sections + per-slice field route ("route B", cohomology-free) | PROCEED | informational (recommended path) |
24	| 3. `[IsAlgClosed kbar]` missing from rigidity chain | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB | major |
25	
26	## Headline answer to the directive's key question
27	
28	**Bridge 2 is NEITHER a 1-iter idiomatic call NOR the hopeless cohomology gap it was feared to
29	be.** It is a **~2–3 iter, cohomology-FREE assembly** via the global-sections / per-closed-point
30	route. The relative "Stein / `f_*O = O`" idiom the directive asked about is **genuinely absent**
31	from Mathlib (no `SteinFactorization`, no proper pushforward connectedness, no `H⁰` proper base
32	change) and would be a multi-iter gap-fill — so **do not frame bridge 2 that way**. The
33	global-sections adjunction + per-slice field argument **does** suffice without `f_*` cohomology,
34	using only facts Mathlib already has, plus one small bespoke globalization step.
35	
36	## Major
37	
38	- **Decision 3 — add `[IsAlgClosed kbar]` to the rigidity chain.** `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`
39	  (L111), `rigidity_core` (L243), `rigidity_lemma` (L324) carry only `[Field kbar]`. The
40	  cohomology-free route needs `κ(closed point) = k̄` (so `Γ(slice)`, a finite extension of `k̄`, is
41	  `k̄`). The downstream consumers `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` (L357) and
42	  `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` (L406) **already** assume `[IsAlgClosed kbar]`; the variable
43	  is literally named `kbar`. **Not** adding it forces the harder generic-fibre route, which needs a
44	  "geometrically connected + geometrically reduced ⟹ `Γ = K`" upgrade over a non-alg-closed base
45	  (separable-and-connected ⟹ trivial-finite-extension) that Mathlib does not package. This is a
46	  low-cost, in-proposal signature change (the bodies are still `sorry`), hence "major", not
47	  must-fix-shipped.
48	
49	## Informational
50	
51	### Decision 1 — the relative Stein / `f_* O = O` framing is a Mathlib gap (avoid it)
52	Searched `Stein factorization`, `proper pushforward structure sheaf`, proper base change for `H⁰`:
53	absent. Only the bare functor `Scheme.Modules.pushforward` / `SheafOfModules.pushforward` exist —
54	no `f_*O = O` connectedness theorem, no `Γ(X ×_S T) = Γ(X) ⊗ Γ(T)`. Building this is the
55	multi-iter coherent-cohomology route the project committed to **avoid** (route (c) was chosen
56	precisely to dodge cohomology). The prover should not attempt to express bridge 2 as `p₂,*O = O`.
57	
58	### Decision 2 — route B: the cohomology-free assembly (recommended). Concrete idiom stack:
59	1. **Per-closed-slice constancy into the affine `U₀`** — `AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isAffine`
60	   (a map into an affine is determined by `appTop`) + `isField_of_universallyClosed` +
61	   `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper`) + alg-closed
62	   triviality of finite extensions. For a **closed** `y ∈ V`, `κ(y)=k̄`, slice `X_y` proper
63	   integral, `Γ(X_y)=k̄`, so `f|X_y` and `(retract ≫ f)|X_y` have equal `appTop : Γ(U₀)→k̄` (same
64	   kernel = the point; a `k̄`-algebra map to `k̄` is pinned by its kernel) ⟹ equal as morphisms.
65	2. **Globalize to `U`** — `ext_of_isDominant` / `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`
66	   (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated`, `IsReduced` source + `IsSeparated` target,
67	   already used by `rigidity_core`) fed a **dense-range** probe. Closed points are dense:
68	   `closure_closedPoints`, `LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace`, `JacobsonSpace`
69	   (`Mathlib.Topology.JacobsonSpace`, `...Morphisms.FiniteType`). Per-point probe:
70	   `Scheme.fromSpecResidueField x` (`range = {x}`). `IsDominant = topologically DenseRange`
71	   (`dominant_eq_topologically`).
72	3. **The single genuinely-missing connective**: a packaged "morphisms agreeing on a **dense set of
73	   closed points** ⟹ equal" hom-ext. Mathlib has only the **single-dominant-morphism**
74	   `ext_of_isDominant`; turning per-closed-point agreement into one dominant probe needs a
75	   `Set`-indexed coproduct `∐_{x ∈ closedPoints U} Spec κ(x) → U` + a `DenseRange` proof, **or** a
76	   small custom Jacobson hom-ext lemma. Buildable from the pieces above; **not** cohomology. This
77	   is the residual ~1–2 iter prover task after Decision 3 is applied.
78	
79	### Note on the directive's other open `sorry` (`hfib`, L154)
80	Out of scope here (a pullback-fibre-topology fact, not the affine-constancy bridge), but the same
81	`Scheme.fromSpecResidueField` / residue-field API is the relevant Mathlib shelf.
82	
83	## Persistent file
84	- `analogies/rigidity-affineconst.md` — full decision blocks + the route-B assembly recipe and
85	  citations, for future iters.
86	
87	Overall verdict: bridge 2 is a cohomology-FREE ~2–3 iter assembly (`ext_of_isAffine` per-slice +
88	`ext_of_isDominant` globalize over dense closed points), **not** the relative `f_*O=O` idiom (a
89	genuine multi-iter Mathlib gap to avoid); first concrete step is adding `[IsAlgClosed kbar]` to the
90	three chain lemmas.
91	</content>
92

- **Bridge 1 (`hfib`)**: solved — a ~10-line char-free assembly via `IsPullback.of_right` + `image_preimage_eq_of_isPullback`, abandoning the heavyweight `Triplet`/tensor route. LSP-verified skeleton in hand.
- **Bridge 2 (agreement equation)**: NOT a hopeless cohomology gap — a **~2–3 iter cohomology-FREE** assembly (per-closed-slice `ext_of_isAffine` + globalize via `ext_of_isDominant` over dense closed points). First concrete step: **add `[IsAlgClosed kbar]`** to the three chain lemmas (downstream consumers already assume it). The relative Stein / `f_*O=O` framing IS a real Mathlib gap — explicitly avoid it.
